preamble: The phonetic transformation from an alveolar nasal /n/ to a voiceless velar plosive /k/ is a specific type of conditioned sound change involving denasalization, devoicing, and a backing of the place of articulation. This shift is typically observed in historical linguistic contexts where an alveolar nasal, often derived from an earlier consonant cluster, loses its nasality and becomes a velar stop.
phoneticEffects: Denasalization, Devoicing, Place Assimilation (Alveolar to Velar), Fortition (Nasal to Plosive)

examples.note: In the word for 'hundred' (百, 'pek' /pɛk/), an earlier medial/final *-nd or *-nt (from Indo-European cognates) is hypothesized to have first assimilated to *-n, which then underwent denasalization to /k/.

In the word for 'hundred' (百, 'hyaku' /hjakɯ/), an earlier medial/final *-nd or *-nt (from Indo-European cognates) is hypothesized to have first assimilated to *-n, which then underwent denasalization to /k/.

sources: Wu, C. H. (2016). Patterns of Sound Correspondence between Taiwanese and Germanic/Latin/Greek/Romance Lexicons. Part I. Sino-Platonic Papers, 262.
